evals.prompt: Design Gate 1 → Gate 2 validation for a bet on AI-native vendor management for mid-market companies.
evals.expected_output: Should structure 60-90 day Gate 1→2 validation. Stage 2 (Problem) work: confirm vendor management is genuinely painful for mid-market (not just an enterprise problem) and customers describe it the way we do. Methods: 10-15 problem-validation interviews with mid-market procurement leaders, switch-interview anchored, focus on circumstance (when does vendor management bite — renewal time, audit, cost-cut quarter, vendor failure). Stage 3 (Solution) work: 3-5 pretotypes testing different solution shapes (Mechanical-Turk concierge, Fake-Door for AI-discovery, Pinocchio for vendor-portal). MVC: a manual concierge experience where humans behind the scenes simulate AI behavior, shipped to 20-30 design-partner customers. Hypothesis test cards for each: hypothesis, success criteria written before test, sample size, decision rule. 14-day cycle target on each test. Output at Gate 2: assumption map updated (top 3 critical assumptions tested), pricing signal from monetization-strategist (initial WTP), advance/pivot/kill recommendation.

evals.prompt: We have 20 features and no product/market fit. Re-run Problem validation.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the symptom: 20 features without product/market fit means the team built solutions to imagined needs (Stage 1→3 jump, skipping Stage 2). Remediation requires resetting back to Problem validation. Structure: (1) Force-stage reset — ban solution discussion for 2 weeks; do only switch interviews and observation; (2) Recruit 12-16 customers including current customers who haven't engaged with the 20 features (their non-use is data) and former customers who churned; (3) Run Problem validation interviews — what was the customer trying to do, what circumstance, what alternatives did they consider, why did they buy us, why don't they engage; (4) Synthesize against the 20 features — which features map to validated jobs, which don't, which are solution-without-problem; (5) Hypothesis: most of the 20 features will not survive Problem validation. Recommendation will likely be ruthless feature reduction (kill 10-15 features), focus on 3-5 that ladder to validated jobs, re-position around those. Hand off pricing reset to monetization-strategist (likely Feature Shock pattern from Monetizing Innovation). Note: this is uncomfortable work because it admits the build was misdirected; coach the team that this is recovery, not failure.

evals.prompt: Build the assumption map for this fintech onboarding bet. Identify the top 3 to test in 30 days. Bet: AI-native onboarding for credit-union members, replacing 45-min in-branch visit with 8-min self-serve.
evals.expected_output: Should structure assumption map on 2x2 (importance × evidence). Likely top assumptions for this bet: (1) [HIGH IMP, LOW EV] Members will trust AI to handle account setup — privacy, identity, regulatory comfort; (2) [HIGH IMP, LOW EV] AI can complete the regulatory checks (KYC, BSA, BOI) without human review for 80%+ of cases — compliance feasibility; (3) [HIGH IMP, LOW EV] 8-min self-serve produces equal or better completion vs. 45-min branch — adoption assumption; (4) [HIGH IMP, MEDIUM EV] Credit unions want this — institutional buyer assumption; (5) [HIGH IMP, LOW EV] AI-driven onboarding maintains member-relationship quality (credit unions are relationship-heavy) — emotional job; (6) [MEDIUM IMP, LOW EV] Pricing model (per-onboarding vs. SaaS subscription) works at scale. Top 3 to test in 30 days: #2 (compliance via regulatory expert + sandbox test), #3 (Mechanical-Turk pretotype with 30-50 prospective members), #5 (qualitative interview signal — credit union staff and members on relationship-vs-self-serve). Each test: hypothesis, method, sample, decision criteria written upfront. Output cycle time target: <14 days per assumption.

evals.prompt: Coach my team through their first pretotype. We've been in MVP for 4 months and have nothing customer-facing.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the symptom: 4 months on MVP with nothing in customer hands means the team is over-engineering before validating. Force a stop. Recommend pivot to pretotyping: (1) Identify the riskiest assumption (typically: will customers actually engage with this concept?); (2) Pick the cheapest pretotype that produces real customer signal — Fake Door (landing page + sign-up that captures intent), Mechanical Turk (humans simulate the algorithm/feature), Pinocchio (non-functional prop), Pretend-to-Own (sell competitor's offering to validate demand); (3) Ship the pretotype within 5 working days — if it takes longer, the pretotype is too elaborate; (4) Define success criteria upfront — sign-ups, conversion, time engaged, money committed; (5) Run for 2-4 weeks; review results; pivot or proceed to actual MVP. Coaching move: pretotyping feels wrong to engineers because it's 'fake.' Reframe: the pretotype is faster validated learning, which is the real product of innovation work at this stage. The actual product can come after the pretotype proves engagement. Reference Savoia's pretotyping framework. Pair with leadership-coach if team resistance to 'fake' is cultural.

evals.prompt: Audit this validation report. Should the bet advance to Gate 2? Report claims: '12 customer interviews completed, 11 confirmed strong interest. NPS would be 65+. Pilot success likely.'
evals.expected_output: Should be skeptical of this report and flag multiple red flags: (1) 11 of 12 customers confirming strong interest is statistically suspicious — typical Stage 2 validation produces mixed signal, not 92% positive; suggests confirmation bias, leading questions, or non-representative recruiting; (2) 'NPS would be 65+' is a projection, not a measurement — what was actually tested? NPS without an actual product to evaluate is fictional; (3) 'Pilot success likely' is conclusion without evidence — pilot success requires Stage 3 (Solution) and Stage 4 (Business Model) validation, not just Stage 2 interviews; (4) No mention of competing solutions, no mention of 'doing nothing' option — likely the customer comparison set is missing; (5) No mention of pricing signal — Stage 4 work hasn't started. Recommendation: do NOT advance to Gate 2. Re-do interviews with adversarial recruitment (customers who said no, competitors' customers, status-quo customers), neutral question framing (no leading questions), and pretotyping to test actual engagement (not just stated interest). If the team pushes back, run the Method Validator's force-stage reset before any further investment.

evals.prompt: We've validated the problem and built an MVC. Now what? Walk me through Stage 3 → Stage 4.
evals.expected_output: Stage 3 to Stage 4 transition: (1) Confirm Stage 3 (Solution) is actually validated — MVC tested with 20-50 target users, behavioral signal (engagement, retention, willingness to commit) confirms preference over alternatives including doing nothing. If Stage 3 isn't validated, do not advance — diagnose the gap; (2) Stage 4 (Business Model) work — pricing, channel, cost structure, partner economics. Methods: WTP studies (van Westendorp at minimum, conjoint preferred for segments), pilot pricing (real money in market), unit-economics math (CAC, LTV, payback, gross margin), channel viability (does the path-to-customer work at scale), cost-to-deliver realistic; (3) Pricing-first principle (Ramanujam & Tacke) — have the WTP conversation before refining the product, not after. Coordinate with monetization-strategist; (4) Stage 4 exit criteria — pricing test passed (target price has segment that buys at scale), unit economics viable (LTV:CAC > 3 typical), launch operating model defined, named operating-BU owner identified for handoff at Gate 3. Common Stage 4 failures: skipping pricing validation (leads to minivation post-launch), missing unit-economics gap (CAC > LTV), no operating model (pilot purgatory). Hand off output to value-engineer for Gate 3 value case.

evals.prompt: Our build-measure-learn cycle is taking 8 weeks per loop. How do I get to 14 days?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose where the 8 weeks are spent. Common patterns: (1) Build phase too elaborate — engineers building production-quality MVP instead of pretotype/MVC; (2) Test phase too small — recruiting 50 customers takes weeks; should pretotype with 5-15 first; (3) Learn phase undisciplined — analysis paralysis on data; should be sharp synthesis sessions on day 1-2 of result review; (4) Decision phase delayed — stakeholders not aligned on advance/pivot/kill criteria upfront. Remediations to compress to 14 days: (a) Build phase ≤ 5 working days — if longer, the pretotype is too elaborate, simplify; (b) Test phase ≤ 5 working days — pretotype with smallest valid sample (often 5-10), recruit through existing networks not formal panels; (c) Learn phase ≤ 2 working days — synthesis session with team on day 1, decision on day 2; (d) Decision criteria written before test runs — eliminates re-litigation; (e) One hypothesis per loop — if testing 3 hypotheses, that's 3 loops in parallel, not one big test. Coach: if the team is resistant to 14-day cycles, the underlying issue may be cultural (perfectionism, fear of fast failure) — coordinate with leadership-coach.

evals.prompt: We've been validating for 7 months and the team is exhausted. The data is mixed — some signals positive, some negative. Should we pivot, persevere, or kill?
evals.expected_output: Should resist a fast answer and structure the decision rigorously: (1) Distinguish data-driven concern from team fatigue — mixed signal at 7 months is normal for H3; team exhaustion is a leadership/cadence issue, not a kill signal by itself; (2) Cumulative learning review — what assumptions have been confirmed, invalidated, still untested? If most critical assumptions are still untested at 7 months, there's a discovery discipline problem (not a bet problem); (3) Strategic shift check — has the market changed? Has a competitor closed off the position? Has internal sponsor changed? These can warrant pivot or kill independent of bet quality; (4) The four-question diagnostic: did we validate critical assumptions? Are next-stage assumptions testable? Has the market changed? Does the team still believe with evidence? Lost-conviction without evidence is fatigue, not data; (5) Recommendation framework: if assumptions invalidated and no path forward → KILL with kill ceremony, redeploy team, capture learning; if assumptions partially validated and a different angle has stronger signal → PIVOT (a strategic change, not iteration); if assumptions validated and the bet is just slow → PERSEVERE with intervention on cadence/team-care; (6) Address team exhaustion separately — even if the bet survives, the team may need rotation, sponsor visibility, or a 2-week reset before the next cycle.
